_meta.name: Her Health Hub — PCOS ModuleLibrary (seed v0)
_meta.status: ILLUSTRATIVE — NOT clinically validated. Do not use with real patients until reviewed_by + validated are set by a clinician.
_meta.source_basis: Modelled on the 2023 International Evidence-Based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of PCOS (Monash / ASRM / ESHRE).
_meta.selection_semantics: Include a module if always=true, OR if ANY trigger in include_when matches the member's intake (symptoms[], primary_goal, conditions[]). The rules engine evaluates include_when; it never invents modules.
_meta.next_step: A panel clinician validates + expands this library, then sets reviewed_by, last_reviewed, validated=true per module.

modules.title: Understand your PCOS
modules.action: Read a 3-minute primer on what PCOS is — and why lifestyle helps, even without weight change.

evidence.claim: Patient education and shared decision-making improve PCOS care.
evidence.rationale: The guideline strongly recommends high-quality patient education and shared decision-making. Understanding the condition is the foundation everything else builds on.

modules.title: Track your cycle & top symptom
modules.action: Log your period days and your most bothersome symptom — 30 seconds a day for a week.

evidence.rationale: Tracking turns vague symptoms into a pattern your clinician can act on, and it's the input your plan adapts to each week.

modules.title: Build gentle movement
modules.action: Start with a 20–30 min brisk walk most days, building toward 150–300 min a week.

evidence.claim: Regular aerobic activity improves metabolic health in PCOS.
evidence.rationale: Guideline activity targets (150–300 min/week moderate, or 75–150 vigorous) improve metabolic health and wellbeing — with benefits even without weight loss.

modules.title: Break up sitting
modules.action: Stand or move for a couple of minutes each hour you're sitting.

evidence.claim: Reducing sedentary time provides health benefits.
evidence.rationale: Replacing sedentary time with activity of any intensity — even light — provides health benefits per the guideline.

modules.title: Steady your sleep
modules.action: Aim for consistent sleep and wake times. Note loud snoring or daytime tiredness to raise at your next consult.

evidence.claim: Sleep problems are more common in PCOS and warrant attention.
evidence.rationale: The guideline recognises sleep problems (including sleep apnoea) as more common in PCOS, so steady sleep and screening for issues are part of core care.

modules.title: Protect your emotional wellbeing
modules.action: Try one short stress-reset a day — a 5-minute breathing or wind-down practice.

evidence.claim: Emotional wellbeing is core to PCOS care.
evidence.rationale: Anxiety and low mood are very common in PCOS. The guideline makes emotional wellbeing and quality of life core to care, not an afterthought.

modules.title: Quick mood check-ins
modules.action: Answer two short mood questions in your weekly check-in, so we can flag early if you'd benefit from more support.

evidence.claim: Routine screening for anxiety and depression is recommended in PCOS.
evidence.rationale: The guideline recommends routinely screening for anxiety and depression in PCOS. These two questions are how your plan knows when to bring in a human.

modules.title: A sustainable eating pattern
modules.action: Aim for regular, balanced meals you can actually keep up. There's no single “PCOS diet” you have to follow.

evidence.claim: No single diet is superior in PCOS; sustainability matters most.
evidence.rationale: The guideline finds no one diet superior. A pattern you can sustain matters more than any specific regimen — and we frame this around health, never restriction.

modules.title: Add light strength work
modules.action: Two short muscle-strengthening sessions a week — bodyweight counts.

evidence.claim: Strength activity supports an active, metabolically beneficial lifestyle.
evidence.rationale: Strength activity supports the metabolic benefits of an active lifestyle and is consistent with population physical-activity guidance the PCOS guideline aligns to.

modules.title: Skin & hair — what actually helps
modules.action: Lifestyle helps slowly. For faster options, discuss medical treatments with your clinician — we won't prescribe in the app.

evidence.claim: Pharmacological options for skin/hair features are clinician-led.
evidence.rationale: The guideline keeps pharmacological options for skin/hair features clinician-led. The app's job is to route you, not to medicate.

modules.title: Check your metabolic health
modules.action: Ask your clinician about screening your blood sugar and cholesterol.

evidence.claim: Metabolic risk and insulin resistance are recognised features; screening is clinician-led.
evidence.rationale: Insulin resistance and metabolic risk are recognised features of PCOS. Screening decisions sit with your clinician — we make sure they're on the radar.

modules.title: Planning for pregnancy
modules.action: Bring your goal and timeline to a clinician — they'll guide safe, evidence-based options. Keep tracking your cycles meanwhile.

evidence.claim: Fertility management should be safe, evidence-based, and clinician-led.
evidence.rationale: The guideline emphasises safer, cheaper, clinician-led fertility management. The plan prepares you for that conversation rather than replacing it.

modules.title: When to reach out sooner
modules.action: Message your care companion if your symptoms change sharply or you feel unwell — don't wait for your next check-in.

evidence.claim: A clear escalation path is basic clinical safety.
evidence.rationale: A clear escalation path is basic clinical safety. Your plan should always tell you when not to wait.
